title: JTBD Analysis — Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing (NAICS 333318)

sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape
sections.content:
### 1.1 CFJ, Output Types, Offering Level

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS** | 333318 |
| **CFJ** | Manufacture and deploy water-using commercial machinery that cleans, treats, purifies, or dispenses aqueous fluids for commercial and institutional operations [SRC: VN_other_commercial_and_service_industry_ma] |
| **Offering Level** | L5 (Product) — standalone commercial equipment |

| Output Type | VN Level |
|---|---|
| Commercial washer-extractor | L5 |
| Continuous tunnel washer | L5 |
| Commercial dryer / ironer | L5 (dry process — no flow sensing) |
| Tunnel car wash system | L5 |
| In-bay automatic car wash | L5 |
| Self-service car wash bay | L5 |
| Commercial water purification system (RO/UV) | L5 |
| Commercial water softener | L5 (pre-treatment only) |
| Commercial ice machine | L5 |
| Beverage vending / dispensing machine | L5 |

### 1.2 Segments (by circumstance)

| # | Segment | Circumstance | Alternatives Differ? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High-volume vended laundry** | Self-service laundromat; 10+ machines running 8–14 cycles/day; pay-per-use billing requires per-cycle water metering; harshest consumer abuse; highest downtime cost | Yes — pay-per-use billing demands metered consumption; no operator present to detect faults |
| 2 | **On-premises institutional laundry** | Hotel, hospital, prison, nursing home; captive use; regulatory hygiene standards (HLAC, TRSA); water audit and sustainability reporting required; dedicated operator on shift | Yes — compliance documentation and water-use benchmarking (10–15 L/kg target) drive sensor accuracy requirements [SRC: web_search — Girbau, Ecolab] |
| 3 | **Express tunnel car wash** | 100–400 vehicles/day; water recycling mandatory in many jurisdictions; speed critical (3–5 min/vehicle); continuous high-volume operation; 50–80% water reclaim typical [SRC: web_search — NCS, EPA WaterSense] | Yes — recycled water quality variability (TDS, turbidity) stresses sensor robustness |
| 4 | **In-bay / self-service car wash** | Customer-operated or automatic single-bay; moderate volume; payment-metered; often unmanned; lower water volume per wash (15–19 gal with reclaim) [SRC: web_search — EPA] | Yes — unmanned operation demands autonomous fault detection |
| 5 | **Commercial water purification** | Restaurant, office, healthcare; food safety compliance (NSF 61); potable water quality; lower flow rates (1–10 l/min); filter saturation and membrane fouling detection critical | Yes — purity requirements and NSF/FDA compliance change alternative selection |
| 6 | **Commercial ice & beverage dispensing** | Food service; hygiene-critical; water consumption tracking for cost control; seasonal demand variation; small pipe diameters (DN12–DN15) | Yes — small bore and intermittent flow challenge sensor low-end range |

### 1.3 Process Value Network

Eight L6 domains (from VN file):

| L6 | Domain | Type | Marquardt Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| L6.1 | Water Supply & Pre-Treatment | Core | Indirect — flow data verifies inlet supply adequacy |
| **L6.2** | **Fluid Circuit & Distribution** | **Core** | **Direct — sensor sits here at L4.2.4.1** |
| L6.3 | Thermal Management | Core | Indirect — flow × ΔT = thermal energy transfer |
| L6.4 | Chemical Dosing & Application | Core | Adjacent — dosing accuracy depends on known flow rate |
| L6.5 | Core Process Execution | Core | Indirect — wash/spray quality depends on flow delivery |
| L6.6 | Drainage, Recovery & Recycling | Core | Adjacent — reclaim flow measurement for water balance |
| L6.7 | Control, Automation & Payment | Core | Direct — sensor feeds controller and IoT data |
| L6.8 | Safety, Compliance & Service | Horizontal | Direct — water efficiency certification requires measured data |

### 1.4–1.5 Product BOM and Edges

Full BOM hierarchy documented in VN file (119 units across L7→L4). Key edges relevant to flow sensing:

| BOM Position | Process Step | Edge Type | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Circulation Pump (L5.2.1) | L6.2 Fluid Circuit & Distribution | **HARD** | Pump capacity sets flow range; sensor must match |
| Solenoid Valve Array (L4.2.3.1) | L6.2 Fluid Circuit & Distribution | **SOFT** | Valve sequencing affects flow profiles but not sensor category |
| High-Pressure Pump (L5.2.2) | L6.2 Fluid Circuit & Distribution | **HARD** | Car wash high-pressure circuits (50–150 bar) exceed sensor rating — different measurement point required |
| Machine Controller / PLC (L5.7.1) | L6.7 Control & Payment | **HARD** | Controller protocol (UART/LIN/frequency) determines sensor interface requirement |
| Water Recycling System (L5.6.2) | L6.6 Drainage & Recycling | **SOFT** | Reclaim water quality affects measurement accuracy but not sensor category |

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Route** | PROCESS_VN — sensor is equipment used to MAKE the customer's output (commercial machinery); it does not end up in the end-customer's hands |
| **Anchor Unit** | Ultrasonic Flow Sensor (L4.2.4.1) |
| **Anchor Level** | L4 (component/subsystem) |
| **Parent VN Unit** | Flow Sensing & Measurement (L5.2.4) |
| **Parent Level** | L5 |
| **Grandparent** | Fluid Circuit & Distribution (L6.2) |
| **L-Path** | L7 Commercial Water-Using Machinery Ecosystem > L6.2 Fluid Circuit & Distribution > L5.2.4 Flow Sensing & Measurement > L4.2.4.1 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor |

### 2.2 Lens Formation

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Route** | Maker — PRODUCT_BOM (sensor becomes part of the commercial machine, which IS the OEM's product) |
| **Function Verb** | Measure (from BOM position function: what the component DOES within the parent system) |
| **Lens** | "What [measurement] does this [fluid circuit & distribution system] need to perform its job?" |

**Segment filtering:**

| Segment | Level A (VN serves?) | Level B (Product modality?) | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| High-volume vended laundry | Yes | Yes — DN14/DN20, water + detergent, 5–85 °C | **Active** |
| On-premises institutional laundry | Yes | Yes — same specs, higher duty cycle | **Active** |
| Express tunnel car wash | Yes | Partial — low-pressure circuits only (DN20/DN25); high-pressure spray >10 bar excluded | **Active (with caveat)** |
| In-bay / self-service car wash | Yes | Yes — low-pressure rinse/reclaim circuits within spec | **Active** |
| Commercial water purification | Yes | Yes — clean water, DN12–DN20, low pressure | **Active** |
| Commercial ice & beverage | Yes | Partial — DN12 in development; very low flow rates near minimum detection | **Active (with caveat)** |

### 2.3 Product Job Statement

> **"Help Flow Sensing & Measurement to measure volumetric flow rate in commercial water-using machinery fluid circuits without obstructing flow or introducing maintenance-prone moving parts"**

Test: 3+ alternatives from different categories? Ultrasonic (acoustic), Turbine/paddle-wheel (mechanical), Electromagnetic (inductive), Solenoid-valve dosing (inferred), Do nothing — **PASS**.

### 2.4 Alternatives (5)

| # | Alternative | UNSPSC | Inherent Trade-Offs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Turbine / Paddle-Wheel Flow Sensor** (incumbent) | 41112501 | Moving rotor clogs from lint, detergent residue, and mineral scale in commercial wash water; bearing wear causes calibration drift ±5%/year; requires periodic manual cleaning; $5–15 unit cost but 1–3 year effective life in harsh media [SRC: web_search — KEYENCE, ICON Process Controls] |
| 2 | **Ultrasonic Flow Sensor** (transit-time) | 41112501 | Accuracy degrades with >2% air entrainment by volume (detergent foam, recycled water aeration); requires pipe-full condition; higher unit cost vs turbine (3–5x at OEM volume); requires fluid-type configuration at commissioning [SRC: 01_product_decomposition] |
| 3 | **Electromagnetic Flowmeter** | 41112501 | Highest accuracy (±0.5%) but 5–10x cost vs turbine; requires power supply (no battery mode); larger installation footprint; conductive fluid required (water qualifies); over-specified for most commercial equipment applications [SRC: web_search — Lanry Instruments] |
| 4 | **Solenoid-Valve Volumetric Dosing** (timed fill) | — | Zero sensor hardware cost; accuracy only ±10–20%; degrades with supply pressure variation; cannot detect leaks, blockages, or anomalies; no consumption data for billing or reporting; budget fallback in price-sensitive equipment [SRC: VN_other_commercial_and_service_industry_ma] |
| 5 | **Do nothing / no measurement** [Status Quo] | — | Zero cost, zero P1 lifecycle burden; water consumption unknown; cannot support pay-per-use billing; cannot meet WaterSense/Energy Star water efficiency reporting; faults and leaks undetectable; water waste invisible |

**Merge test:** Each alternative creates distinct P1 lifecycle burdens — turbine has cleaning/replacement cycles, ultrasonic has configuration/acoustic concerns, electromagnetic has cost/power concerns, solenoid dosing and do-nothing have zero measurement burden but zero detection capability. No merge needed.

### 2.5 Scenarios and Output Type Activation

| Scenario | Activated Output Types | BOM Positions in Play |
|---|---|---|
| **S1: Vended laundry per-cycle billing** | Commercial washer-extractor | Circulation Pump, Solenoid Valve Array, Flow Sensor, Payment System |
| **S2: Tunnel car wash water reclaim monitoring** | Tunnel car wash system | Circulation Pump, Water Recycling System, Flow Sensor, Effluent Flow Meter |
| **S3: Institutional laundry water audit** | Continuous tunnel washer | Circulation Pump, Flow Sensor, IoT & Connectivity, Chemical Dispensing |
| **S4: Water purification filter monitoring** | Commercial water purification (RO/UV) | Pre-Filtration, RO Membrane, Flow Sensor, Controller |
| **S5: Ice machine consumption tracking** | Commercial ice machine | Water Inlet Assembly, Flow Sensor, Controller |
| **S6: In-bay car wash leak detection** | In-bay automatic car wash | High-Pressure Pump, Flow Sensor, Leak Detection, Controller |

Sparsity check: S4 and S5 activate different output types; S2 uniquely activates recycling-related BOM. **PASS** — not every scenario activates every type.

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### 3.0 Stakeholder Roles for This Market

| Role | Who (in NAICS 333318 context) |
|---|---|
| **Job Executor** | OEM assembly technician / field service installer — integrates and installs the sensor in the machine |
| **Job Overseer** | OEM product engineering manager — accountable for machine performance, water efficiency, and certification |
| **Job Beneficiary** | Laundromat owner, hotel facility manager, car wash operator — receives water-use data and operational reliability |
| **Product Lifecycle Support** | Specifier (OEM design engineer), assembler, commissioning tech, field service technician, disposer |
| **Purchase Executor** | OEM procurement manager — selects and sources sensor components for production |
| **Purchase Influencer** | Water utility auditor, EPA/WaterSense certifier, franchise compliance officer |

### 3.1 Job Map (8 Universal Steps — Ulwick 2005)

| # | Step | Statement | Relevant to Product? | Relevance Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DEFINE** | Define the required flow rate range, pipe diameter, media compatibility (water + detergent + recycled water), temperature envelope, and communication protocol for the machine's fluid circuit | **Yes** | Sensor specifications must match the machine's diverse operating conditions across output types |
| 2 | **LOCATE** | Locate the optimal insertion point in the machine's internal piping where flow profile is developed and space constraints of the machine enclosure are met | **Yes** | Commercial machines have compact enclosures; sensor body size and straight-pipe requirements directly affect packaging feasibility |
| 3 | **PREPARE** | Prepare the pipe section, electrical connections (UART/LIN/frequency), and controller interface for sensor integration on the OEM assembly line | **Yes** | Mechanical and electrical integration into high-volume OEM production is core to product adoption |
| 4 | **CONFIRM** | Confirm sensor readiness at end-of-line: verify pipe-full condition, absence of air pockets, correct wiring, and communication handshake with machine controller | **Yes** | Pre-shipment quality validation is critical — field failures in vended/unmanned equipment are costly |
| 5 | **EXECUTE** | Execute continuous flow measurement, providing real-time volumetric flow rate, consumption totals, temperature, and diagnostic flags to the machine controller | **Yes** | This IS the core value-creating action of the product |
| 6 | **MONITOR** | Monitor measurement quality indicators: signal strength, air bubble events, empty-pipe alarms, flow anomalies indicating leaks or blockages | **Yes** | Ongoing measurement integrity enables IoT water monitoring, predictive maintenance, and leak detection |
| 7 | **MODIFY** | Adjust or recalibrate if measurement deviates — re-zero, update fluid properties, replace sensor | No | Ultrasonic sensors require minimal in-service adjustment; commercial equipment is rarely field-recalibrated |
| 8 | **CONCLUDE** | Conclude service life: decommission sensor during machine disposal or recycling | No | Standard end-of-life; not a differentiating step for flow sensors in commercial equipment |

**Relevant steps: 6 of 8** (Steps 1–6). Steps 7–8 not relevant to product differentiation.

### 3.2 P1 + P2 Needs: Error Statements

#### P2 — Core Job Needs (solution-independent)

| ID | Job Step | Error Type | Statement | Importance | Satisfaction | Opp. Score | Impact | Confidence | product_related |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N01 | EXECUTE | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of volumetric flow reading deviating more than ±3% from true value when measuring detergent-laden or chemically treated water across the 0.5–50 l/min operating range | 9 | 4 | 14 | reliability | 0.8 | false |
| N02 | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize measurement degradation when water contains suspended detergent residues, lint particles, or chemical dosing additives at concentrations typical of commercial wash cycles | 8 | 3 | 13 | reliability | 0.8 | false |
| N03 | EXECUTE | CONSISTENCY | Minimize variability of water consumption readings between identical wash/rinse cycles under the same programme settings (repeatability >±1%) | 8 | 5 | 11 | reliability | 0.7 | false |
| N04 | MONITOR | DETECTION | Minimize time to detect a water supply failure or pipe-empty condition to less than 5 seconds to prevent dry-run damage to pumps and heating elements | 9 | 4 | 14 | safety | 0.8 | false |
| N05 | EXECUTE | THROUGHPUT | Increase the ability to measure across the full machine flow range (trickle-fill 0.5 l/min to high-volume rinse 40 l/min) in a single sensor without changing hardware | 7 | 5 | 9 | reliability | 0.7 | false |
| N06 | MONITOR | DETECTION | Minimize the number of undetected water leaks within the machine's internal fluid circuit that waste more than 50 litres per day | 8 | 3 | 13 | cost | 0.7 | false |
| N07 | DEFINE | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of specifying a sensor whose pressure rating or media compatibility is incompatible with the machine's operating conditions across its full output-type range | 7 | 5 | 9 | cost | 0.6 | false |
| N08 | EXECUTE | ACCURACY | Increase the ability to measure water consumption per cycle with sufficient accuracy (±2%) to support pay-per-use billing models and per-machine water-use reporting | 8 | 3 | 13 | cost | 0.8 | false |
| N09 | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize measurement failure when water temperature ranges from 5 °C (cold fill) to 85 °C (hot wash cycle) within a single machine operating envelope | 7 | 5 | 9 | reliability | 0.7 | false |

#### P1 — Product Jobs per Alternative (Lifecycle Stages)

**Alternative 1: Turbine / Paddle-Wheel Flow Sensor (incumbent)**

| ID | Lifecycle Stage | Error Type | Statement | Imp. | Sat. | Opp. | Impact | Conf. | product_related |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N10 | Usage | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize the likelihood of flow sensor rotor clogging from lint, detergent residue, or mineral scale within 12 months of installation in commercial laundry or car wash equipment [SRC: web_search — KEYENCE, ICON Process Controls] | 8 | 2 | 14 | reliability | 0.9 | true |
| N11 | Maintenance | EFFORT | Minimize the frequency of manual sensor disassembly and cleaning required to prevent accumulated debris from degrading flow measurement accuracy below ±5% | 7 | 3 | 11 | cost | 0.8 | true |
| N12 | Usage | CONSISTENCY | Minimize calibration drift exceeding ±5% per year caused by mechanical wear of paddle-wheel bearings and blade erosion in detergent-laden water [SRC: web_search — KEYENCE] | 8 | 3 | 13 | reliability | 0.8 | true |
| N12b | Usage | THROUGHPUT | Increase the ability to maintain accurate readings at low flow rates (<2 l/min) where turbine/paddle-wheel rotors stall due to insufficient fluid momentum to overcome mechanical inertia | 7 | 3 | 11 | reliability | 0.7 | true |

**Alternative 2: Ultrasonic Flow Sensor (transit-time)**

| ID | Lifecycle Stage | Error Type | Statement | Imp. | Sat. | Opp. | Impact | Conf. | product_related |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N13 | Preparation | EFFORT | Minimize the OEM integration time required to configure fluid-type parameters and verify acoustic coupling for each machine variant during assembly-line commissioning | 6 | 5 | 7 | time | 0.7 | true |
| N14 | Usage | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize the likelihood of signal loss when air bubbles from detergent agitation or water recycling aeration pass through the sensor during normal operation | 7 | 4 | 10 | reliability | 0.8 | true |
| N14b | Acquisition | EFFORT | Minimize the OEM qualification cost for validating acoustic performance across multiple machine platforms with different pipe diameters (DN14–DN25) and media compositions | 6 | 4 | 8 | cost | 0.6 | true |
| N14c | Usage | DETECTION | Increase the ability to distinguish genuine zero-flow conditions from ultrasonic signal loss caused by detergent foam or entrained air from recycled water systems | 8 | 3 | 13 | safety | 0.7 | true |

**Alternative 4: Solenoid-Valve Volumetric Dosing (timed fill)**

| ID | Lifecycle Stage | Error Type | Statement | Imp. | Sat. | Opp. | Impact | Conf. | product_related |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N15 | Usage | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of water fill volume deviating more than ±15% from target due to supply pressure variations that invalidate timed-fill calibration | 7 | 2 | 12 | reliability | 0.7 | true |
| N15b | Usage | DETECTION | Minimize the number of undetected water circuit faults (partial blockage, stuck valve, internal leak) that timed-fill dosing cannot sense because it lacks direct flow measurement | 8 | 2 | 14 | safety | 0.7 | true |

#### P2 — Additional Core Job Needs (Executor + Overseer)

| ID | Job Step | Error Type | Statement | Imp. | Sat. | Opp. | Impact | Conf. | product_related |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N16 | LOCATE | EFFORT | Minimize the required installation envelope (sensor body length and straight-pipe clearance) to fit within space-constrained commercial machine enclosures without requiring piping redesign | 7 | 5 | 9 | cost | 0.7 | false |

| N19 | EXECUTE | ACCURACY | Increase the ability to provide real-time per-cycle water consumption data to the machine controller for IoT dashboard reporting and water-efficiency compliance documentation [SRC: web_search — Speed Queen Insights, EPA WaterSense] | 8 | 3 | 13 | cost | 0.8 | false |
| N20 | MONITOR | DETECTION | Increase the ability to detect abnormal water consumption patterns (e.g., >20% above baseline for the same cycle programme) indicating component degradation, valve failure, or internal leak | 7 | 3 | 11 | reliability | 0.6 | false |
| N21 | DEFINE | EFFORT | Minimize the number of sensor variants an OEM must qualify and stock to cover its full product range of water-using equipment across DN12–DN25 pipe sizes | 6 | 4 | 8 | cost | 0.6 | false |
| N22 | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize the likelihood of flow measurement failure when water recycling systems introduce variable water quality (TDS 200–2,000 ppm, turbidity, temperature cycling) into the machine supply [SRC: web_search — NCS, Sensorex] | 8 | 3 | 13 | reliability | 0.8 | false |

#### P2 — Overseer Needs (OEM Engineering Manager)

| ID | Job Step | Error Type | Statement | Imp. | Sat. | Opp. | Impact | Conf. | product_related |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N23 | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize the field failure rate of the flow sensing subsystem to below 0.2% within the 10-year commercial machine design life | 9 | 5 | 13 | reliability | 0.8 | false |
| N24 | DEFINE | ACCURACY | Increase the ability to document measured water consumption per cycle for EPA WaterSense, Energy Star, or EU Ecodesign water efficiency certification using onboard sensor data [SRC: web_search — EPA WaterSense] | 8 | 3 | 13 | cost | 0.7 | false |

### 3.3 P3–P5 Needs: Bridge Questions

sections.title: QA Validation
sections.content:
| # | Check | Result |
|---|---|---|

rows.Value: Manufacture and deploy water-using commercial machinery that cleans, treats, purifies, or dispenses aqueous fluids for commercial and institutional operations [SRC: VN_other_commercial_and_service_industry_ma]

rows.Circumstance: Self-service laundromat; 10+ machines running 8–14 cycles/day; pay-per-use billing requires per-cycle water metering; harshest consumer abuse; highest downtime cost
rows.Alternatives Differ?: Yes — pay-per-use billing demands metered consumption; no operator present to detect faults

rows.Circumstance: Hotel, hospital, prison, nursing home; captive use; regulatory hygiene standards (HLAC, TRSA); water audit and sustainability reporting required; dedicated operator on shift
rows.Alternatives Differ?: Yes — compliance documentation and water-use benchmarking (10–15 L/kg target) drive sensor accuracy requirements [SRC: web_search — Girbau, Ecolab]

rows.Circumstance: 100–400 vehicles/day; water recycling mandatory in many jurisdictions; speed critical (3–5 min/vehicle); continuous high-volume operation; 50–80% water reclaim typical [SRC: web_search — NCS, EPA WaterSense]
rows.Alternatives Differ?: Yes — recycled water quality variability (TDS, turbidity) stresses sensor robustness

rows.Circumstance: Customer-operated or automatic single-bay; moderate volume; payment-metered; often unmanned; lower water volume per wash (15–19 gal with reclaim) [SRC: web_search — EPA]
rows.Alternatives Differ?: Yes — unmanned operation demands autonomous fault detection

rows.Circumstance: Restaurant, office, healthcare; food safety compliance (NSF 61); potable water quality; lower flow rates (1–10 l/min); filter saturation and membrane fouling detection critical
rows.Alternatives Differ?: Yes — purity requirements and NSF/FDA compliance change alternative selection

rows.Circumstance: Food service; hygiene-critical; water consumption tracking for cost control; seasonal demand variation; small pipe diameters (DN12–DN15)
rows.Alternatives Differ?: Yes — small bore and intermittent flow challenge sensor low-end range

rows.Marquardt Relevance: Indirect — flow data verifies inlet supply adequacy
Indirect — flow × ΔT = thermal energy transfer
Adjacent — dosing accuracy depends on known flow rate
Indirect — wash/spray quality depends on flow delivery
Adjacent — reclaim flow measurement for water balance
Direct — sensor feeds controller and IoT data
Direct — water efficiency certification requires measured data

rows.Rationale: Pump capacity sets flow range; sensor must match
Valve sequencing affects flow profiles but not sensor category
Car wash high-pressure circuits (50–150 bar) exceed sensor rating — different measurement point required
Controller protocol (UART/LIN/frequency) determines sensor interface requirement
Reclaim water quality affects measurement accuracy but not sensor category

rows.Value: PROCESS_VN — sensor is equipment used to MAKE the customer's output (commercial machinery); it does not end up in the end-customer's hands
L7 Commercial Water-Using Machinery Ecosystem > L6.2 Fluid Circuit & Distribution > L5.2.4 Flow Sensing & Measurement > L4.2.4.1 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor
Maker — PRODUCT_BOM (sensor becomes part of the commercial machine, which IS the OEM's product)
Measure (from BOM position function: what the component DOES within the parent system)
"What [measurement] does this [fluid circuit & distribution system] need to perform its job?"

rows.Level B (Product modality?): Yes — same specs, higher duty cycle
Partial — low-pressure circuits only (DN20/DN25); high-pressure spray >10 bar excluded
Yes — low-pressure rinse/reclaim circuits within spec
Yes — clean water, DN12–DN20, low pressure
Partial — DN12 in development; very low flow rates near minimum detection

rows.Inherent Trade-Offs: Moving rotor clogs from lint, detergent residue, and mineral scale in commercial wash water; bearing wear causes calibration drift ±5%/year; requires periodic manual cleaning; $5–15 unit cost but 1–3 year effective life in harsh media [SRC: web_search — KEYENCE, ICON Process Controls]

Accuracy degrades with >2% air entrainment by volume (detergent foam, recycled water aeration); requires pipe-full condition; higher unit cost vs turbine (3–5x at OEM volume); requires fluid-type configuration at commissioning [SRC: 01_product_decomposition]

Highest accuracy (±0.5%) but 5–10x cost vs turbine; requires power supply (no battery mode); larger installation footprint; conductive fluid required (water qualifies); over-specified for most commercial equipment applications [SRC: web_search — Lanry Instruments]

Zero sensor hardware cost; accuracy only ±10–20%; degrades with supply pressure variation; cannot detect leaks, blockages, or anomalies; no consumption data for billing or reporting; budget fallback in price-sensitive equipment [SRC: VN_other_commercial_and_service_industry_ma]

Zero cost, zero P1 lifecycle burden; water consumption unknown; cannot support pay-per-use billing; cannot meet WaterSense/Energy Star water efficiency reporting; faults and leaks undetectable; water waste invisible

Circulation Pump, Flow Sensor, IoT & Connectivity, Chemical Dispensing

Water Inlet Assembly, Flow Sensor, Controller

rows.Who (in NAICS 333318 context): OEM assembly technician / field service installer — integrates and installs the sensor in the machine
OEM product engineering manager — accountable for machine performance, water efficiency, and certification
Laundromat owner, hotel facility manager, car wash operator — receives water-use data and operational reliability
Specifier (OEM design engineer), assembler, commissioning tech, field service technician, disposer
OEM procurement manager — selects and sources sensor components for production
Water utility auditor, EPA/WaterSense certifier, franchise compliance officer

rows.Statement: Define the required flow rate range, pipe diameter, media compatibility (water + detergent + recycled water), temperature envelope, and communication protocol for the machine's fluid circuit
rows.Relevance Rationale: Sensor specifications must match the machine's diverse operating conditions across output types

rows.Statement: Locate the optimal insertion point in the machine's internal piping where flow profile is developed and space constraints of the machine enclosure are met
rows.Relevance Rationale: Commercial machines have compact enclosures; sensor body size and straight-pipe requirements directly affect packaging feasibility

rows.Statement: Prepare the pipe section, electrical connections (UART/LIN/frequency), and controller interface for sensor integration on the OEM assembly line
rows.Relevance Rationale: Mechanical and electrical integration into high-volume OEM production is core to product adoption

rows.Statement: Confirm sensor readiness at end-of-line: verify pipe-full condition, absence of air pockets, correct wiring, and communication handshake with machine controller
rows.Relevance Rationale: Pre-shipment quality validation is critical — field failures in vended/unmanned equipment are costly

rows.Statement: Execute continuous flow measurement, providing real-time volumetric flow rate, consumption totals, temperature, and diagnostic flags to the machine controller
rows.Relevance Rationale: This IS the core value-creating action of the product

rows.Statement: Monitor measurement quality indicators: signal strength, air bubble events, empty-pipe alarms, flow anomalies indicating leaks or blockages
rows.Relevance Rationale: Ongoing measurement integrity enables IoT water monitoring, predictive maintenance, and leak detection

rows.Statement: Adjust or recalibrate if measurement deviates — re-zero, update fluid properties, replace sensor
rows.Relevance Rationale: Ultrasonic sensors require minimal in-service adjustment; commercial equipment is rarely field-recalibrated

rows.Statement: Conclude service life: decommission sensor during machine disposal or recycling
rows.Relevance Rationale: Standard end-of-life; not a differentiating step for flow sensors in commercial equipment

rows.Statement: Minimize the likelihood of volumetric flow reading deviating more than ±3% from true value when measuring detergent-laden or chemically treated water across the 0.5–50 l/min operating range
Minimize measurement degradation when water contains suspended detergent residues, lint particles, or chemical dosing additives at concentrations typical of commercial wash cycles
Minimize variability of water consumption readings between identical wash/rinse cycles under the same programme settings (repeatability >±1%)
Minimize time to detect a water supply failure or pipe-empty condition to less than 5 seconds to prevent dry-run damage to pumps and heating elements
Increase the ability to measure across the full machine flow range (trickle-fill 0.5 l/min to high-volume rinse 40 l/min) in a single sensor without changing hardware
Minimize the number of undetected water leaks within the machine's internal fluid circuit that waste more than 50 litres per day
Minimize the likelihood of specifying a sensor whose pressure rating or media compatibility is incompatible with the machine's operating conditions across its full output-type range
Increase the ability to measure water consumption per cycle with sufficient accuracy (±2%) to support pay-per-use billing models and per-machine water-use reporting
Minimize measurement failure when water temperature ranges from 5 °C (cold fill) to 85 °C (hot wash cycle) within a single machine operating envelope

Minimize the likelihood of flow sensor rotor clogging from lint, detergent residue, or mineral scale within 12 months of installation in commercial laundry or car wash equipment [SRC: web_search — KEYENCE, ICON Process Controls]
Minimize the frequency of manual sensor disassembly and cleaning required to prevent accumulated debris from degrading flow measurement accuracy below ±5%
Minimize calibration drift exceeding ±5% per year caused by mechanical wear of paddle-wheel bearings and blade erosion in detergent-laden water [SRC: web_search — KEYENCE]
Increase the ability to maintain accurate readings at low flow rates (<2 l/min) where turbine/paddle-wheel rotors stall due to insufficient fluid momentum to overcome mechanical inertia
Minimize the OEM integration time required to configure fluid-type parameters and verify acoustic coupling for each machine variant during assembly-line commissioning
Minimize the likelihood of signal loss when air bubbles from detergent agitation or water recycling aeration pass through the sensor during normal operation
Minimize the OEM qualification cost for validating acoustic performance across multiple machine platforms with different pipe diameters (DN14–DN25) and media compositions
Increase the ability to distinguish genuine zero-flow conditions from ultrasonic signal loss caused by detergent foam or entrained air from recycled water systems
Minimize the likelihood of water fill volume deviating more than ±15% from target due to supply pressure variations that invalidate timed-fill calibration
Minimize the number of undetected water circuit faults (partial blockage, stuck valve, internal leak) that timed-fill dosing cannot sense because it lacks direct flow measurement
Minimize the required installation envelope (sensor body length and straight-pipe clearance) to fit within space-constrained commercial machine enclosures without requiring piping redesign
Minimize the likelihood of mounting the sensor at a location where turbulence from upstream valves, elbows, or T-junctions causes more than 2% systematic reading bias
Minimize the total OEM assembly-line integration time from unpacking to first valid reading, including mechanical fitting, electrical connection, and protocol handshake
Minimize the likelihood of a sensor passing end-of-line quality test while having an undetected installation defect (misalignment, air pocket, reversed orientation) that causes field failure
Minimize the likelihood of releasing a machine from end-of-line testing with a flow sensor calibration error exceeding ±3% that goes undetected during the automated test cycle

Increase the ability to provide real-time per-cycle water consumption data to the machine controller for IoT dashboard reporting and water-efficiency compliance documentation [SRC: web_search — Speed Queen Insights, EPA WaterSense]
Increase the ability to detect abnormal water consumption patterns (e.g., >20% above baseline for the same cycle programme) indicating component degradation, valve failure, or internal leak
Minimize the number of sensor variants an OEM must qualify and stock to cover its full product range of water-using equipment across DN12–DN25 pipe sizes

Minimize the likelihood of flow measurement failure when water recycling systems introduce variable water quality (TDS 200–2,000 ppm, turbidity, temperature cycling) into the machine supply [SRC: web_search — NCS, Sensorex]
Minimize the field failure rate of the flow sensing subsystem to below 0.2% within the 10-year commercial machine design life

Increase the ability to document measured water consumption per cycle for EPA WaterSense, Energy Star, or EU Ecodesign water efficiency certification using onboard sensor data [SRC: web_search — EPA WaterSense]

entities.naics_title: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
entities.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
entities.cfj_statement: Manufacture and deploy water-using commercial machinery that cleans, treats, purifies, or dispenses aqueous fluids for commercial and institutional operations

job_map.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
job_map.product_job_statement: Help Flow Sensing & Measurement to measure volumetric flow rate in commercial water-using machinery fluid circuits without obstructing flow or introducing maintenance-prone moving parts

steps.statement: Define the required flow rate range, pipe diameter, media compatibility, temperature envelope, and communication protocol for the machine's fluid circuit
steps.relevance_rationale: Sensor specifications must match the machine's diverse operating conditions across output types

steps.statement: Locate the optimal insertion point in the machine's internal piping where flow profile is developed and space constraints of the machine enclosure are met
steps.relevance_rationale: Commercial machines have compact enclosures; sensor body size and straight-pipe requirements directly affect packaging feasibility

steps.statement: Prepare the pipe section, electrical connections, and controller interface for sensor integration on the OEM assembly line
steps.relevance_rationale: Mechanical and electrical integration into high-volume OEM production is core to product adoption

steps.statement: Confirm sensor readiness at end-of-line: verify pipe-full condition, absence of air pockets, correct wiring, and communication handshake with machine controller
steps.relevance_rationale: Pre-shipment quality validation is critical — field failures in vended/unmanned equipment are costly

steps.statement: Execute continuous flow measurement, providing real-time volumetric flow rate, consumption totals, temperature, and diagnostic flags to the machine controller
steps.relevance_rationale: This IS the core value-creating action of the product

steps.statement: Monitor measurement quality indicators: signal strength, air bubble events, empty-pipe alarms, flow anomalies indicating leaks or blockages
steps.relevance_rationale: Ongoing measurement integrity enables IoT water monitoring, predictive maintenance, and leak detection

steps.statement: Adjust or recalibrate if measurement deviates — re-zero, update fluid properties, replace sensor
steps.relevance_rationale: Ultrasonic sensors require minimal in-service adjustment; commercial equipment is rarely field-recalibrated

steps.statement: Conclude service life: decommission sensor during machine disposal or recycling
steps.relevance_rationale: Standard end-of-life; not a differentiating step

alternatives.name: Turbine / Paddle-Wheel Flow Sensor
trade_offs: Moving rotor clogs from lint, detergent residue, and mineral scale
Bearing wear causes calibration drift ±5%/year
Requires periodic manual disassembly and cleaning
$5-15 unit cost but 1-3 year effective life in harsh media
Low-flow dead band below ~2 l/min due to rotor inertia

alternatives.name: Ultrasonic Flow Sensor (transit-time)
trade_offs: Accuracy degrades with >2% air entrainment by volume (detergent foam)
Requires pipe-full condition for valid measurement
Higher unit cost vs turbine (3-5x at OEM volume)
Requires fluid-type configuration at commissioning

alternatives.name: Electromagnetic Flowmeter
trade_offs: Highest accuracy (±0.5%) but 5-10x cost vs turbine
Requires power supply (no battery mode)
Larger installation footprint
Over-specified and over-priced for most commercial equipment

alternatives.name: Solenoid-Valve Volumetric Dosing (timed fill)
trade_offs: Accuracy only ±10-20%
Degrades with supply pressure variation
Cannot detect leaks, blockages, or anomalies
No consumption data for billing or reporting

alternatives.name: Do nothing / no measurement
trade_offs: Zero cost, zero P1 lifecycle burden
Water consumption unknown
Cannot support pay-per-use billing
Cannot meet WaterSense/Energy Star certification
Faults and leaks undetectable

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of volumetric flow reading deviating more than ±3% from true value when measuring detergent-laden or chemically treated water across the 0.5–50 l/min operating range
error_statements.rationale: Commercial wash water contains surfactants, lint, and chemical additives. Accurate flow measurement enables water-use reporting, chemical dosing accuracy, and per-cycle billing. A ±3% flow error propagates directly to ±3% billing/dosing error.

error_statements.statement: Minimize measurement degradation when water contains suspended detergent residues, lint particles, or chemical dosing additives at concentrations typical of commercial wash cycles
error_statements.rationale: Detergent-laden water is the standard operating medium in commercial laundry (surfactant concentration 0.5-2 g/L) and car wash (foam, wax). Turbine sensors clog; any viable sensor must handle contaminated media without degradation.

error_statements.statement: Minimize variability of water consumption readings between identical wash/rinse cycles under the same programme settings (repeatability >±1%)
error_statements.rationale: Pay-per-use billing and water-use benchmarking require repeatable consumption measurements. Poor repeatability causes disputes with vended-laundry operators and invalidates water-efficiency audits.

error_statements.statement: Minimize time to detect a water supply failure or pipe-empty condition to less than 5 seconds to prevent dry-run damage to pumps and heating elements
error_statements.rationale: Dry-run conditions destroy circulation pumps and heating elements within minutes. In unmanned vended/car-wash equipment, no human is present to notice. Rapid empty-pipe detection is a safety-critical requirement.

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to measure across the full machine flow range (trickle-fill 0.5 l/min to high-volume rinse 40 l/min) in a single sensor without changing hardware
error_statements.rationale: Commercial machines run diverse programmes with very different flow rates. A single sensor covering the full range eliminates multi-sensor installations and reduces OEM BOM complexity.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the number of undetected water leaks within the machine's internal fluid circuit that waste more than 50 litres per day
error_statements.rationale: Internal leaks (worn seals, cracked hoses, failed check valves) waste water and can cause property damage. In unmanned equipment, leaks persist for days before detection. A flow sensor can detect non-zero flow during idle periods.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of specifying a sensor whose pressure rating or media compatibility is incompatible with the machine's operating conditions across its full output-type range
error_statements.rationale: OEMs producing multiple machine types (laundry + car wash + water treatment) need a sensor that works across diverse conditions. Mis-specification leads to field failures and warranty claims.

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to measure water consumption per cycle with sufficient accuracy (±2%) to support pay-per-use billing models and per-machine water-use reporting
error_statements.rationale: Pay-per-use vended laundry and car wash models require metered consumption for fair billing. Water-use reporting is increasingly required by franchise operators and sustainability auditors. Timed-fill (±10-20%) is insufficient.

error_statements.statement: Minimize measurement failure when water temperature ranges from 5 °C (cold fill) to 85 °C (hot wash cycle) within a single machine operating envelope
error_statements.rationale: Commercial washers cycle between cold fill and hot wash within minutes. Speed of sound in water changes ~2.4 m/s per °C. Sensors without real-time temperature compensation accumulate error during rapid thermal transients.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of flow sensor rotor clogging from lint, detergent residue, or mineral scale within 12 months of installation in commercial laundry or car wash equipment
error_statements.rationale: Turbine/paddle-wheel rotors in commercial wash water are exposed to lint, surfactant residue, and mineral deposits. Fragments break off from scaling buildup and jam the rotor. Most turbine sensors in commercial laundry fail or drift significantly within 1-2 years.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the frequency of manual sensor disassembly and cleaning required to prevent accumulated debris from degrading flow measurement accuracy below ±5%
error_statements.rationale: Field service calls to clean turbine sensors cost $150-300 per visit. In a fleet of 50+ machines, quarterly cleaning schedules create significant ongoing maintenance expense that erodes total cost of ownership.

error_statements.statement: Minimize calibration drift exceeding ±5% per year caused by mechanical wear of paddle-wheel bearings and blade erosion in detergent-laden water
error_statements.rationale: Paddle-wheel bearings in detergent water experience accelerated wear. Blade erosion from particulates changes the flow-to-pulse relationship. Drift is silent — no alarm indicates gradual accuracy loss — leading to undetected billing and dosing errors.

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to maintain accurate readings at low flow rates (<2 l/min) where turbine/paddle-wheel rotors stall due to insufficient fluid momentum to overcome mechanical inertia
error_statements.rationale: Trickle-fill and pre-rinse cycles operate at low flow rates where mechanical rotors cannot start. This creates a measurement dead zone for turbine sensors, missing water consumption that should be tracked.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the OEM integration time required to configure fluid-type parameters and verify acoustic coupling for each machine variant during assembly-line commissioning
error_statements.rationale: OEM assembly lines target <30 seconds per sensor station. Acoustic coupling verification and fluid-type parameter configuration add setup steps not present with simple pulse-output turbine sensors. Pre-calibrated delivery mitigates this.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of signal loss when air bubbles from detergent agitation or water recycling aeration pass through the sensor during normal operation
error_statements.rationale: Detergent agitation in wash cycles and aeration in water recycling systems introduce air bubbles >2% vol intermittently. Ultrasonic transit-time sensors lose signal during these events. In car wash reclaim systems, aeration is continuous.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the OEM qualification cost for validating acoustic performance across multiple machine platforms with different pipe diameters (DN14–DN25) and media compositions
error_statements.rationale: OEMs producing diverse machine types need to validate sensor performance across each platform. Ultrasonic sensors require acoustic validation per DN variant and fluid type — a qualification matrix that turbine sensors (simple pulse output) do not require.

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to distinguish genuine zero-flow conditions from ultrasonic signal loss caused by detergent foam or entrained air from recycled water systems
error_statements.rationale: In commercial laundry and car wash, the controller must differentiate between an actual supply failure (requiring pump shutdown) and temporary signal loss from foam/bubbles (normal operation). False-positive empty-pipe alarms cause unnecessary cycle interruptions; missed true failures cause pump damage.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of water fill volume deviating more than ±15% from target due to supply pressure variations that invalidate timed-fill calibration
error_statements.rationale: Timed-fill calibration assumes constant supply pressure. In multi-machine installations, simultaneous fill cycles drop pressure 20-40%, causing underfill. Peak demand periods produce opposite overfill. Neither condition is detected.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the number of undetected water circuit faults (partial blockage, stuck valve, internal leak) that timed-fill dosing cannot sense because it lacks direct flow measurement
error_statements.rationale: Solenoid-valve timed fill has zero fault-detection capability. A stuck-open valve, a partial blockage, or an internal leak produces no alarm. The fault persists until a human notices wet floors or abnormal wash results — potentially days in unmanned equipment.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the required installation envelope (sensor body length and straight-pipe clearance) to fit within space-constrained commercial machine enclosures without requiring piping redesign
error_statements.rationale: Commercial machine enclosures are compact and highly optimized. Adding a flow sensor requires physical space for the sensor body plus 5-10x DN straight-pipe run upstream. Smaller sensors with shorter straight-run requirements enable retrofitting without piping redesign.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of mounting the sensor at a location where turbulence from upstream valves, elbows, or T-junctions causes more than 2% systematic reading bias
error_statements.rationale: Machine internal piping is short and dense with frequent directional changes. Ideal straight-run requirements (5-10x DN) are often impossible to meet. Sensors tolerant of disturbed flow profiles are preferred.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the total OEM assembly-line integration time from unpacking to first valid reading, including mechanical fitting, electrical connection, and protocol handshake
error_statements.rationale: Commercial equipment OEMs produce hundreds to thousands of machines per year. Every additional minute of sensor integration time on the assembly line translates to measurable production cost. Plug-and-play sensors with auto-sensing are preferred.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of a sensor passing end-of-line quality test while having an undetected installation defect (misalignment, air pocket, reversed orientation) that causes field failure
error_statements.rationale: End-of-line tests may not catch subtle installation defects. Reversed sensor orientation, for example, gives correct flow magnitude but inverted direction — undetectable in a single-direction test. Built-in self-diagnostics reduce escape rates.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of releasing a machine from end-of-line testing with a flow sensor calibration error exceeding ±3% that goes undetected during the automated test cycle
error_statements.rationale: OEM end-of-line tests typically verify sensor communication and gross function but not calibration accuracy against a traceable reference. Pre-calibrated sensors eliminate this risk at the sensor level.

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to provide real-time per-cycle water consumption data to the machine controller for IoT dashboard reporting and water-efficiency compliance documentation
error_statements.rationale: IoT-enabled commercial equipment (Speed Queen Insights, WashTec SmartCare) demands per-cycle water data for fleet dashboards. Water-efficiency certification (EPA WaterSense, Energy Star) increasingly requires measured — not estimated — consumption data.

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to detect abnormal water consumption patterns (e.g., >20% above baseline for the same cycle programme) indicating component degradation, valve failure, or internal leak
error_statements.rationale: Trend analysis of per-cycle consumption enables predictive maintenance — detecting valve degradation, seal wear, or developing leaks before they become catastrophic. Requires baseline measurement capability that timed-fill systems lack.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the number of sensor variants an OEM must qualify and stock to cover its full product range of water-using equipment across DN12–DN25 pipe sizes
error_statements.rationale: OEMs producing multiple machine types prefer a single sensor platform with interchangeable flow bodies. Marquardt's shared-electronics platform across DN variants directly addresses this. Fewer part numbers reduce procurement, warehousing, and quality management overhead.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of flow measurement failure when water recycling systems introduce variable water quality (TDS 200–2,000 ppm, turbidity, temperature cycling) into the machine supply
error_statements.rationale: Car wash water recycling systems reclaim 50-80% of water. Reclaimed water has higher TDS, variable turbidity, and temperature cycling that challenge sensor robustness. Any sensor deployed downstream of reclaim must tolerate this variability.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the field failure rate of the flow sensing subsystem to below 0.2% within the 10-year commercial machine design life
error_statements.rationale: Commercial equipment OEMs target 10-year appliance design life with <0.5% warranty claim rate for any component. Flow sensor failure in unmanned vended equipment causes revenue loss and expensive field service dispatches.

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to document measured water consumption per cycle for EPA WaterSense, Energy Star, or EU Ecodesign water efficiency certification using onboard sensor data
error_statements.rationale: EPA WaterSense and Energy Star water-efficiency labels are increasingly required for institutional procurement and franchise compliance. Measured per-cycle data from onboard sensors provides certification evidence that estimated data cannot.

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to demonstrate measured water efficiency data that qualifies the equipment for WaterSense or equivalent eco-labels on the first certification submission
error_statements.rationale: P3 Role Identity: The OEM engineer whose equipment consistently earns sustainability certifications. Measured flow data enables first-pass certification, avoiding costly resubmissions.

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to provide per-cycle water usage data visible on the machine HMI or IoT dashboard that commercial customers can include in sustainability reporting
error_statements.rationale: P4 Image Identity: Franchise operators and facility managers are watching. They need verifiable water-use data for ESG reporting. Equipment that provides transparent, measured data builds brand trust.

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of a facility operator receiving an unexpected water utility bill exceeding budget by more than 15% due to undetected equipment water waste that the sensor could have flagged
error_statements.rationale: P5 Emotional [hypothesis]: Facility managers want to feel confident water costs are controlled. Unexpected utility spikes cause anxiety and erode trust in equipment. Continuous flow monitoring provides early warning of waste.
